paragraphs.text: Dear reader, I write this in the hope that my feedback will impact the digital space, as I find these topics important. Initiatives around dark patterns in online interfaces, addictive design, personalized targeting, the difficulty of managing digital subscriptions, and problematic practices by social media influencers are all issues I care deeply about. I would have much more to say on these if it werent for the 4000-character limit, which is unfortunate, but I understand. Everything written as feedback will be my own opinion, which should not be taken as anything but allegedly or entertainment. Therefore, Id like to focus on one digital practice I feel most strongly about: companies revoking access to media Ive purchased. This ties into the Stop Killing Games initiative Ive already signed, but Ill start with a personal story. Ive been a huge fan of video games my whole life. As a child, some of my fondest memories are of learning new languages through games with my parents and gaming together as a family. Video games have had a lasting impact on me, and as a teen, The Sims 2 was one of my all-time favorites. I had many - if not all - of the game's CD copies, received as gifts over the years. But as technology moved on and CD drives disappeared from PCs, I gradually stopped playing. Some years ago, EA (owner of The Sims franchise) released the full Sims 2 game on its game launcher. I downloaded it, as did many others. But a few years later, during the popularity of The Sims 3 and 4, some of us noticed The Sims 2 had vanished from our libraries. When some users contacted EA support, they were told the company had stopped supporting the game and removed it - and were advised to play the newer Sims 4 instead. This moment was the straw that broke the camels back for me. Thats why the "Stop Killing Games" movement is so important to me. Having media I purchased removed, only to be told to buy a newer version, feels not only unethical but like adding insult to injury - especially when no laws protect us from this happening again. When technology evolves and formats like CDs are phased out, we lose traditional ways of preserving media. But the alternative where companies can remove digital access at will is no solution either. This is why I believe new games should come with a clear plan for what happens when theyre no longer supported. If I buy a game, I shouldnt lose my personal copy just because its no longer profitable to the company. Let the community take over. And if thats not possible, then force them to be transparent: make them clarify youll only rent the game. Another troubling practice is pay to remove personalized ads. Ive been targeted by personalized ads multiple times - and the only time I succumbed; it was a scam, on Instagram. I reported it to Meta, and they did nothing. Recently, Meta introduced a new dilemma: pay to opt out of personalized ads or accept them. In my opinion, this is unethical and goes against the spirit of GDPR. It undermines the idea that I have control over my data, unless I pay for that right. I urge you to take this seriously and ensure such practices arent allowed, especially by massive corporations as powerful as Meta. Lastly, Id also like to mention the strong influence American payment processors have over European purchasing power in the digital space - but unfortunately, Ive run out of space. I will however say I wish there would be stronger pushback from the EU, and that EU would take stronger initiative to protect the consumer rights within EU and make sure that consumers and companies wouldn't be "bullied around" by the payment processors demanding Europeans follow American laws, which often feels to be extremely unfair and unjust towards the consumers while protecting the corporations. To reiterate, everything written is my own opinion. But, I hope youll consider what Ive written with an open mind. Thank you for your time and consideration.
